inputs.question: Management of severe hyperglycemia (blood glucose >600 mg/dL) in adults: differential diagnosis between DKA, HHS, and simple severe hyperglycemia. What is the guideline-recommended insulin dosing for hyperglycemic emergencies per ADA 2024 hyperglycemic crises consensus and JBDS-IP DKA/HHS guidelines? Is a fixed 30 units regular insulin IV bolus appropriate, versus 0.1 U/kg/hr infusion with or without 0.1 U/kg bolus? What are the risks of large insulin bolus including hypoglycemia, intracellular potassium shift causing hypokalemia, and cerebral edema?

output.text:
Differential diagnosis of severe hyperglycemia (>600 mg/dL) distinguishes between DKA, HHS, and simple severe hyperglycemia based on the presence of ketosis and acidosis. DKA requires hyperglycemia (typically >250 mg/dL, though euglycemic DKA exists), pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15-18 mEq/L, and positive ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate >3.0 mmol/L). HHS presents with more severe hyperglycemia (>600 mg/dL), pH >7.3, bicarbonate >15 mEq/L, minimal or absent ketones, and hyperosmolality. Simple severe hyperglycemia lacks both significant ketosis and acidosis.[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] Guideline-recommended insulin dosing from the ADA 2024 consensus and current standards favors continuous IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr without an initial bolus for adults with DKA and HHS. The JBDS-IP guidelines recommend a lower fixed-rate infusion of 0.05 units/kg/hr for HHS, increasing by 1 unit/hr as needed.[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$Umpierrez GE, Davis GM, ElSayed NA, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults With Diabetes: A Consensus Report. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(8):1257-1275. doi:10.2337/dci24-0032.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$Lin R, Wootton E, Gaca M, et al. Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State: A Systematic Review of Management Guidelines and Their Evidence. Diabetic Medicine : A Journal of the British Diabetic Association. 2026;:e70226. doi:10.1111/dme.70226.$$$]]] A fixed 30-unit IV bolus is not appropriate and is not recommended by current guidelines, which have moved away from bolus dosing in favor of continuous infusion started 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation.[[[$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] Risks of large insulin boluses include hypoglycemia, severe hypokalemia from rapid intracellular potassium shift, and potentially cerebral edema (particularly in pediatric populations, though the mechanism remains debated). Current protocols emphasize starting insulin infusion only after adequate fluid resuscitation and ensuring potassium is >3.3 mEq/L before initiating insulin to mitigate hypokalemia risk.[[[$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. Diagnostic Criteria

The diagnostic criteria differentiate these conditions based on specific laboratory parameters:[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] DKA (all criteria must be met): - Glucose >250 mg/dL (euglycemic DKA: <200 mg/dL with known diabetes) - pH <7.3 (arterial or venous) - Bicarbonate <15-18 mEq/L - Anion gap >10-12 mEq/L - Positive urine or serum ketones - Beta-hydroxybutyrate >3.0 mmol/L HHS: - Glucose >600 mg/dL - pH >7.3 - Bicarbonate >15 mEq/L - Trace or negative ketones - Serum osmolality typically >320 mOsm/kg - Often altered mental status or coma Simple severe hyperglycemia: - Glucose >600 mg/dL - No significant acidosis (pH >7.3) - No significant ketosis - Normal or mildly elevated anion gap Approximately 10% of DKA cases present as euglycemic DKA (glucose <200 mg/dL), often associated with SGLT2 inhibitor use, pregnancy, reduced food intake, or alcohol use.[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]]] Mixed DKA-HHS presentations occur in approximately 10% of cases and carry higher mortality than either condition alone.[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]]]

Insulin Dosing Recommendations

ADA 2024 Consensus and Current Standards (2026): For DKA in adults:[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] - No initial IV bolus is recommended - Start continuous IV regular insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr - Begin insulin 1-2 hours after initiating fluid resuscitation - Ensure potassium >3.3 mEq/L before starting insulin - Target glucose reduction of ~50 mg/dL/hr and bicarbonate increase of ~3 mEq/L/hr - When glucose reaches 250 mg/dL, decrease infusion to 0.05 units/kg/hr and add dextrose to IV fluids - Continue insulin infusion until DKA resolution (pH >7.3, bicarbonate ≥18 mEq/L, anion gap normalized) For HHS:[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$Lin R, Wootton E, Gaca M, et al. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$Rao P, Jiang SF, Kipnis P, et al. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]]] - Administer basal insulin (glargine) 2-4 hours before stopping IV insulin - Continue IV insulin for 30-60 minutes after first subcutaneous rapid-acting dose - Early administration of basal insulin (0.2-0.3 units/kg) during IV infusion may prevent rebound hyperglycemia

A fixed 30-unit IV bolus is inappropriate because it: 1. Causes severe hypoglycemia:[[[$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] - Excessive insulin drives glucose down too rapidly - Risk increases when glucose reaches 250 mg/dL but ketoacidosis persists - Current protocols mandate decreasing insulin to 0.05 units/kg/hr at glucose 250 mg/dL specifically to prevent hypoglycemia 2. Precipitates dangerous hypokalemia:[[[$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] - Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, causing rapid serum potassium decline - Patients with DKA/HHS have total body potassium depletion despite initially normal or elevated serum levels - Large boluses cause precipitous drops in serum potassium - Guidelines require potassium >3.3 mEq/L before starting insulin and mandate holding insulin if potassium falls below normal range - Potassium monitoring every 2 hours (hourly if abnormal) is standard during treatment - Hypokalemia risk is why insulin is decreased to 0.05 units/kg/hr when glucose reaches 250 mg/dL 3. May increase cerebral edema risk:[[[$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] - Cerebral edema is a rare but serious complication, particularly in pediatric DKA - Rapid osmolality changes from aggressive insulin and fluid therapy may contribute - Mechanism remains debated but includes rapid glucose correction causing osmotic shifts - Current protocols emphasize gradual correction with target glucose reduction of ~50 mg/dL/hr 4. Does not improve outcomes:[[[$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. Fluid resuscitation is the priority:[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] - Begin with 15-20 mL/kg/hr or 1-1.5 L/hr of 0.9% normal saline - JBDS recommends replacing ~50% of estimated fluid deficit within first 12 hours - Insulin should be delayed 1-2 hours after starting fluids Electrolyte monitoring and replacement:[[[$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.$$$]]] - Add potassium to IV fluids once levels are below upper limit of normal and urine output established - Monitor potassium every 2 hours (hourly if abnormal) - Bicarbonate not routinely recommended - Phosphorus and magnesium replacement only for severe deficiencies Resolution criteria for DKA:[[[$$$American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.$$$]!!![$$$Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. follow_up_questions: Which patient populations are most at risk for complications from high-dose insulin boluses?
What are the guideline recommendations for insulin dosing adjustments in elderly or renal impairment?
What monitoring protocols are advised for early detection of cerebral edema during insulin therapy?
structured_article.raw_text:
**Differential diagnosis** of severe hyperglycemia (>600 mg/dL) distinguishes between **DKA, HHS, and simple severe hyperglycemia** based on the presence of ketosis and acidosis. **DKA** requires hyperglycemia (typically >250 mg/dL, though euglycemic DKA exists), pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15-18 mEq/L, and positive ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate >3.0 mmol/L). **HHS** presents with more severe hyperglycemia (>600 mg/dL), pH >7.3, bicarbonate >15 mEq/L, minimal or absent ketones, and hyperosmolality. **Simple severe hyperglycemia** lacks both significant ketosis and acidosis[1][2][4].

**Guideline-recommended insulin dosing** from the **ADA 2024 consensus** and current standards favors **continuous IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr without an initial bolus** for adults with DKA and HHS. The **JBDS-IP guidelines** recommend a **lower fixed-rate infusion of 0.05 units/kg/hr** for HHS, increasing by 1 unit/hr as needed[7][8][9][12]. A **fixed 30-unit IV bolus is not appropriate** and is not recommended by current guidelines, which have moved away from bolus dosing in favor of continuous infusion started 1-2 hours after fluid resuscitation[9][11][14].

**Risks of large insulin boluses** include **hypoglycemia, severe hypokalemia** from rapid intracellular potassium shift, and potentially **cerebral edema** (particularly in pediatric populations, though the mechanism remains debated). Current protocols emphasize starting insulin infusion only after adequate fluid resuscitation and ensuring potassium is >3.3 mEq/L before initiating insulin to mitigate hypokalemia risk[9][13][14].

<visual>Figure 3[6]</visual>

## Diagnostic Criteria

The diagnostic criteria differentiate these conditions based on specific laboratory parameters[1][2][4]:

**DKA (all criteria must be met):**
- Glucose >250 mg/dL (euglycemic DKA: <200 mg/dL with known diabetes)
- pH <7.3 (arterial or venous)
- Bicarbonate <15-18 mEq/L
- Anion gap >10-12 mEq/L
- Positive urine or serum ketones
- Beta-hydroxybutyrate >3.0 mmol/L

**HHS:**
- Glucose >600 mg/dL
- pH >7.3
- Bicarbonate >15 mEq/L
- Trace or negative ketones
- Serum osmolality typically >320 mOsm/kg
- Often altered mental status or coma

**Simple severe hyperglycemia:**
- Glucose >600 mg/dL
- No significant acidosis (pH >7.3)
- No significant ketosis
- Normal or mildly elevated anion gap

Approximately 10% of DKA cases present as euglycemic DKA (glucose <200 mg/dL), often associated with SGLT2 inhibitor use, pregnancy, reduced food intake, or alcohol use[1][7]. Mixed DKA-HHS presentations occur in approximately 10% of cases and carry higher mortality than either condition alone[1][7].

## Insulin Dosing Recommendations

**ADA 2024 Consensus and Current Standards (2026):**

For **DKA in adults**[7][9][11][14]:
- **No initial IV bolus** is recommended
- Start **continuous IV regular insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr**
- Begin insulin **1-2 hours after initiating fluid resuscitation**
- Ensure potassium >3.3 mEq/L before starting insulin
- Target glucose reduction of ~50 mg/dL/hr and bicarbonate increase of ~3 mEq/L/hr
- When glucose reaches 250 mg/dL, **decrease infusion to 0.05 units/kg/hr** and add dextrose to IV fluids
- Continue insulin infusion until DKA resolution (pH >7.3, bicarbonate ≥18 mEq/L, anion gap normalized)

For **HHS**[7][12]:
- **ADA/Diabetes Canada**: 0.1 units/kg/hr fixed-rate infusion
- **JBDS-IP**: 0.05 units/kg/hr, increasing by 1 unit/hr if targets not met
- Lower initial rates reflect concerns about rapid osmolality changes

**Alternative subcutaneous protocols** for mild-to-moderate DKA[9][11][18]:
- Rapid-acting insulin (lispro/aspart): 0.3 units/kg bolus, then 0.1 units/kg hourly
- Can be used in emergency department or step-down units
- Requires aggressive fluid management and frequent monitoring
- May reduce ICU admissions and nursing workload

**Transition to subcutaneous insulin**[9][11]:
- Administer basal insulin (glargine) 2-4 hours before stopping IV insulin
- Continue IV insulin for 30-60 minutes after first subcutaneous rapid-acting dose
- Early administration of basal insulin (0.2-0.3 units/kg) during IV infusion may prevent rebound hyperglycemia

## Risks of Large Insulin Boluses

**A fixed 30-unit IV bolus is inappropriate** because it:

**1. Causes severe hypoglycemia**[9][13][14]:
- Excessive insulin drives glucose down too rapidly
- Risk increases when glucose reaches 250 mg/dL but ketoacidosis persists
- Current protocols mandate decreasing insulin to 0.05 units/kg/hr at glucose 250 mg/dL specifically to prevent hypoglycemia

**2. Precipitates dangerous hypokalemia**[9][13][14]:
- Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, causing rapid serum potassium decline
- Patients with DKA/HHS have total body potassium depletion despite initially normal or elevated serum levels
- Large boluses cause precipitous drops in serum potassium
- Guidelines require potassium >3.3 mEq/L before starting insulin and mandate holding insulin if potassium falls below normal range
- Potassium monitoring every 2 hours (hourly if abnormal) is standard during treatment
- Hypokalemia risk is why insulin is decreased to 0.05 units/kg/hr when glucose reaches 250 mg/dL

**3. May increase cerebral edema risk**[13]:
- Cerebral edema is a rare but serious complication, particularly in pediatric DKA
- Rapid osmolality changes from aggressive insulin and fluid therapy may contribute
- Mechanism remains debated but includes rapid glucose correction causing osmotic shifts
- Current protocols emphasize gradual correction with target glucose reduction of ~50 mg/dL/hr

**4. Does not improve outcomes**[9][11][15]:
- Historical practice included 0.1 units/kg IV bolus followed by infusion
- Current evidence shows **no benefit to bolus dosing**
- Continuous infusion without bolus is now standard practice
- Fixed-rate infusion provides more predictable, controlled correction

## Management Principles

**Fluid resuscitation is the priority**[1][7][14]:
- Begin with 15-20 mL/kg/hr or 1-1.5 L/hr of 0.9% normal saline
- JBDS recommends replacing ~50% of estimated fluid deficit within first 12 hours
- Insulin should be delayed 1-2 hours after starting fluids

**Electrolyte monitoring and replacement**[9][13]:
- Add potassium to IV fluids once levels are below upper limit of normal and urine output established
- Monitor potassium every 2 hours (hourly if abnormal)
- Bicarbonate not routinely recommended
- Phosphorus and magnesium replacement only for severe deficiencies

**Resolution criteria for DKA**[1][9]:
- pH >7.3
- Bicarbonate ≥18 mEq/L
- Anion gap normalized
- Glucose <200 mg/dL
- Clinical improvement

Would you like to explore the specific management differences between mixed DKA-HHS presentations versus pure DKA or HHS, including how to adjust insulin and fluid protocols for these overlapping conditions?

articlespan_set.text: The diagnostic criteria differentiate these conditions based on specific laboratory parameters:

articlespan_set.text: - Glucose >250 mg/dL (euglycemic DKA: <200 mg/dL with known diabetes)
- Often altered mental status or coma
- Normal or mildly elevated anion gap
Approximately 10% of DKA cases present as euglycemic DKA (glucose <200 mg/dL), often associated with SGLT2 inhibitor use, pregnancy, reduced food intake, or alcohol use.

articlespan_set.text: Mixed DKA-HHS presentations occur in approximately 10% of cases and carry higher mortality than either condition alone.

articlespan_set.text: - Start continuous IV regular insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr
- Begin insulin 1-2 hours after initiating fluid resuscitation
- Ensure potassium >3.3 mEq/L before starting insulin
- Target glucose reduction of ~50 mg/dL/hr and bicarbonate increase of ~3 mEq/L/hr
- When glucose reaches 250 mg/dL, decrease infusion to 0.05 units/kg/hr and add dextrose to IV fluids
- Continue insulin infusion until DKA resolution (pH >7.3, bicarbonate ≥18 mEq/L, anion gap normalized)

articlespan_set.text: - JBDS-IP: 0.05 units/kg/hr, increasing by 1 unit/hr if targets not met
- Lower initial rates reflect concerns about rapid osmolality changes

citation_detail.title: Evaluation of Outcomes Following Hospital-Wide Implementation of a Subcutaneous Insulin Protocol for Diabetic Ketoacidosis
citation_detail.authors_string: Rao P, Jiang SF, Kipnis P, et al.

citations.citation: Rao P, Jiang SF, Kipnis P, et al. Evaluation of Outcomes Following Hospital-Wide Implementation of a Subcutaneous Insulin Protocol for Diabetic Ketoacidosis. JAMA Network Open. 2022;5(4):e226417. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2022.6417.

articlespan_set.text: - Rapid-acting insulin (lispro/aspart): 0.3 units/kg bolus, then 0.1 units/kg hourly
- Can be used in emergency department or step-down units
- Requires aggressive fluid management and frequent monitoring
- May reduce ICU admissions and nursing workload

citations.citation: Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.

citation_detail.title: 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026
citation_detail.authors_string: American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*.

citations.citation: American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Supplement_1):S339-S355. doi:10.2337/dc26-S016.

articlespan_set.text: - Administer basal insulin (glargine) 2-4 hours before stopping IV insulin
- Continue IV insulin for 30-60 minutes after first subcutaneous rapid-acting dose
- Early administration of basal insulin (0.2-0.3 units/kg) during IV infusion may prevent rebound hyperglycemia

citations.citation: Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.
Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.

articlespan_set.text: - Excessive insulin drives glucose down too rapidly
- Risk increases when glucose reaches 250 mg/dL but ketoacidosis persists
- Current protocols mandate decreasing insulin to 0.05 units/kg/hr at glucose 250 mg/dL specifically to prevent hypoglycemia

citations.citation: Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.
Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.
Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.

articlespan_set.text: - Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, causing rapid serum potassium decline
- Patients with DKA/HHS have total body potassium depletion despite initially normal or elevated serum levels
- Large boluses cause precipitous drops in serum potassium
- Guidelines require potassium >3.3 mEq/L before starting insulin and mandate holding insulin if potassium falls below normal range
- Potassium monitoring every 2 hours (hourly if abnormal) is standard during treatment
- Hypokalemia risk is why insulin is decreased to 0.05 units/kg/hr when glucose reaches 250 mg/dL

citations.citation: Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.

articlespan_set.text: - Cerebral edema is a rare but serious complication, particularly in pediatric DKA
- Rapid osmolality changes from aggressive insulin and fluid therapy may contribute
- Mechanism remains debated but includes rapid glucose correction causing osmotic shifts
- Current protocols emphasize gradual correction with target glucose reduction of ~50 mg/dL/hr

citations.citation: Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.

citation_detail.authors_string: Karslioglu French E, Donihi AC, Korytkowski MT.

articlespan_set.text: - Historical practice included 0.1 units/kg IV bolus followed by infusion
- Current evidence shows no benefit to bolus dosing
- Continuous infusion without bolus is now standard practice
- Fixed-rate infusion provides more predictable, controlled correction

citation_detail.title: 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2026
citation_detail.authors_string: American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee for Diabetes*.

articlespan_set.text: - Begin with 15-20 mL/kg/hr or 1-1.5 L/hr of 0.9% normal saline
- JBDS recommends replacing ~50% of estimated fluid deficit within first 12 hours
- Insulin should be delayed 1-2 hours after starting fluids

citations.citation: Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.
Veauthier B, Levy-Grau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024;110(5):476-486.

articlespan_set.text: - Add potassium to IV fluids once levels are below upper limit of normal and urine output established
- Monitor potassium every 2 hours (hourly if abnormal)
- Phosphorus and magnesium replacement only for severe deficiencies
